article.title: Anthropic Buys All of SpaceX's Colossus 1 Capacity in Memphis, Adding 300 Megawatts and 220,000 GPUs to Claude's Inference Fleet
article.summary: The deal between former rivals gives Anthropic the entire 300 MW Memphis supercomputer that xAI built in 2024, immediately doubling Claude Code rate limits and removing peak-hours throttling.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Anthropic and SpaceX announced on May 6 that Anthropic will take over the entire compute capacity of Colossus 1, the Memphis-area supercomputer originally built by xAI in 2024. According to [Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/news/higher-limits-spacex), the agreement gives Claude "more than 300 megawatts of new capacity (over 220,000 NVIDIA GPUs) within the month," and the company is using the additional resources to immediately raise customer-facing limits. The deal puts a former vocal critic of Anthropic in the position of selling it the bulk of an AI training cluster that, until earlier this year, was the home of Grok.

## What We Know

**Capacity and location.** Colossus 1 sits near Memphis, Tennessee. [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/06/anthropic-use-spacexs-colossus-1-supercomputer-inference/) reports that the facility was originally built by xAI Holdings Corp. in 2024 and that SpaceX acquired xAI earlier in 2026. The site delivers 300 megawatts of power and houses more than 220,000 Nvidia GPUs, figures confirmed in both [Anthropic's announcement](https://www.anthropic.com/news/higher-limits-spacex) and [Fortune's](https://fortune.com/2026/05/07/big-tech-will-spend-nearly-700-billion-on-ai-this-year-no-one-knows-where-the-buildout-ends/) coverage.

**What Anthropic is doing with the cluster.** [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/06/anthropic-use-spacexs-colossus-1-supercomputer-inference/) reports that Anthropic will use Colossus 1 to power Claude for Pro and Max subscription tiers, with the 300 megawatts allocated for Claude inference rather than frontier-model training. [Anthropic's own announcement](https://www.anthropic.com/news/higher-limits-spacex) frames the same point in product terms: the new capacity "will directly improve capacity for Claude Pro and Claude Max subscribers."

**Immediate product effects.** [Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/news/higher-limits-spacex) says it is "doubling Claude Code's five-hour rate limits" for Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise customers and "removing the peak hours limit reduction" for Pro and Max accounts. API rate limits for Claude Opus models have also been raised. [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/06/anthropic-use-spacexs-colossus-1-supercomputer-inference/) describes the API change as letting users process significantly more input and output tokens per minute.

**Orbital ambition.** [Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/news/higher-limits-spacex) writes that, alongside the Colossus 1 deal, the company has "expressed interest in partnering with SpaceX to develop multiple gigawatts of orbital AI compute capacity." The phrasing falls short of a binding commitment but signals that Anthropic, like several rivals, is treating space-based data centers as an option for the next decade rather than a science-fiction footnote.

**xAI's side of the trade.** [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/06/is-xai-a-neocloud-now/) reports that xAI has shifted its training operations to Colossus 2, a newer site adjacent to Colossus 1, and that Musk explained the sale of Colossus 1 by saying xAI "didn't need them both." The same article calls the Colossus 1 transaction "likely worth billions of dollars," though no party has disclosed terms. [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/06/anthropic-use-spacexs-colossus-1-supercomputer-inference/) reports that Colossus 2 is expected to provide 2 gigawatts of power at full capacity.

**Anthropic's other compute partners.** [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/06/anthropic-use-spacexs-colossus-1-supercomputer-inference/) notes that the SpaceX agreement sits alongside Anthropic's earlier deals: about 1 gigawatt with Amazon by year-end 2026, scaling up to 4 gigawatts long-term under a $25 billion investment announced in April, and a similarly sized deal with Google and Broadcom for capacity coming online in 2027.

## What We Don't Know

Neither company has disclosed the financial terms of the Colossus 1 deal. [TechCrunch's](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/06/is-xai-a-neocloud-now/) "likely worth billions of dollars" is an outside estimate, not a figure either side has confirmed. The exact mix of GPU generations inside Colossus 1 has not been publicly broken down by either party, and the timeline beyond "within the month" for full Anthropic access has not been spelled out. The orbital-compute language in Anthropic's announcement is interest, not a contract.

## Analysis

The partnership is striking less for its scale than for its participants. [Fortune](https://fortune.com/2026/05/07/big-tech-will-spend-nearly-700-billion-on-ai-this-year-no-one-knows-where-the-buildout-ends/) recounts that Musk wrote earlier this year that Anthropic "hates Western civilization," yet weeks later his company is the one renting Anthropic the silicon to run Claude. [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/06/is-xai-a-neocloud-now/) frames the realignment as turning xAI into a "neocloud" — a company that buys GPUs from Nvidia and rents the resulting capacity to model developers — and notes that Grok has seen "plummeting usage" through 2026 even as xAI's January funding round set a $230 billion valuation. Selling a 300 MW data center to a former rival is one way to monetize that capacity in the meantime.

For Anthropic, the deal shortens the lag between commercial demand and capacity. Pro and Max users have been the most vocal about throttled usage; bringing Colossus 1 online "within the month," per [Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/news/higher-limits-spacex), is the fastest path to easing those limits without waiting for the multi-year Amazon and Google buildouts to come fully online.
